This helps performance on both the client and the server. This configuration controls the \\\"\\n84:                                                  + \\\"default batch size in bytes. \\\"\\n85:                                                  + \\\"<p>\\\"\\n86:                                                  + \\\"No attempt will be made to batch records larger than this size. \\\"\\n87:                                                  + \\\"<p>\\\"\\n88:                                                  + \\\"Requests sent to brokers will contain multiple batches, one for each partition with data available to be sent. \\\"\\n89:                                                  + \\\"<p>\\\"\\n90:                                                  + \\\"A small batch size will make batching less common and may reduce throughput (a batch size of zero will disable \\\"\\n91:                                                  + \\\"batching entirely). A very large batch size may use memory a bit more wastefully as we will always allocate a \\\"\\n92:                                                  + \\\"buffer of the specified batch size in anticipation of additional records.\\\"\\n93:                                                  + \\\"<p>\\\"\\n94:                                                  + \\\"Note: This setting gives the upper bound of the batch size to be sent. If we have fewer than this many bytes accumulated \\\"\\n95:                                                  + \\\"for this partition, we will 'linger' for the <code>linger.ms</code> time waiting for more records to show up. \\\"\\n96:                                                  + \\\"This <code>linger.ms</code> setting defaults to 5, which means the producer will wait for 5ms or until the record batch is \\\"\\n97:                                                  + \\\"of <code>batch.size</code> (whichever happens first) before sending the record batch. This controls the \\\"\\n125:                                            + \\\" durability of records that are sent. The following settings are allowed: \\\"\\n126:                                            + \\\" <ul>\\\"\\n127:                                            + \\\" <li><code>acks=0</code> If set to zero then the producer will not wait for any acknowledgment from the\\\"\\n128:                                            + \\\" server at all. The record will be immediately added to the socket buffer and considered sent. No guarantee can be\\\"\\n129:                                            + \\\" made that the server has received the record in this case, and the <code>retries</code> configuration will not\\\"\\n130:                                            + \\\" take effect (as the client won't generally know of any failures). The offset given back for each record will\\\"\\n131:                                            + \\\" always be set to <code>-1</code>.\\\"\\n132:                                            + \\\" <li><code>acks=1</code> This will mean the leader will write the record to its local log but will respond\\\"\\n133:                                            + \\\" without awaiting full acknowledgement from all followers. In this case should the leader fail immediately after\\\"\\n134:                                            + \\\" acknowledging the record but before the followers have replicated it then the record will be lost.\\\"\\n135:                                            + \\\" <li><code>acks=all</code> This means the leader will wait for the full set of in-sync replicas to\\\"\\n136:                                            + \\\" acknowledge the record. This guarantees that the record will not be lost as long as at least one in-sync replica\\\"\\n137:                                            + \\\" remains alive. This is the strongest available guarantee. This is equivalent to the acks=-1 setting.\\\"\\n138:                                            + \\\"</ul>\\\"\\n139:                                            + \\\"<p>\\\"\\n140:                                            + \\\"Note that enabling idempotence requires this config value to be 'all'.\\\"\\n141:                                            + \\\" If conflicting configurations are set and idempotence is not explicitly enabled, idempotence is disabled.\\\";\\n142: \\n143:     /** <code>linger.ms</code> */\\n144:     public static final String LINGER_MS_CONFIG = \\\"linger.ms\\\";\\n145:     private static final String LINGER_MS_DOC = \\\"The producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. \\\"\\n146:                                                 + \\\"Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they can be sent out. However in some circumstances the client may want to \\\"\\n147:                                                 + \\\"reduce the number of requests even under moderate load. This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount \\\"\\n148:                                                 + \\\"of artificial delay&mdash;that is, rather than immediately sending out a record, the producer will wait for up to \\\"\\n149:                                                 + \\\"the given delay to allow other records to be sent so that the sends can be batched together. This can be thought \\\"\\n150:                                                 + \\\"of as analogous to Nagle's algorithm in TCP. This setting gives the upper bound on the delay for batching: once \\\"\\n151:                                                 + \\\"we get <code>\\\" + BATCH_SIZE_CONFIG + \\\"</code> worth of records for a partition it will be sent immediately regardless of this \\\"\\n152:                                                 + \\\"setting, however if we have fewer than this many bytes accumulated for this partition we will 'linger' for the \\\"\\n153:                                                 + \\\"specified time waiting for more records to show up. Increasing <code>\\\" + LINGER_MS_CONFIG + \\\"=50</code>, \\\"\\n154:                                                 + \\\"for example, would have the effect of reducing the number of requests sent but would add up to 50ms of latency to records sent in the absence of load.\\\"\\n155:                                                 + \\\"The default changed from 0 to 5 in Apache Kafka 4.0 as the efficiency gains from larger batches typically result in \\\"\\n156:                                                 + \\\"similar or lower producer latency despite the increased linger.\\\";\\n157: \\n158:     /** <code>request.timeout.ms</code> */\\n159:     public static final String REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG = CommonClientConfigs.REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG;\\n160:     private static final String REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC = CommonClientConfigs.REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC\\n161:         + \\\" This should be larger than <code>replica.lag.time.max.ms</code> (a broker configuration)\\\"\\n162:         + \\\" to reduce the possibility of message duplication due to unnecessary producer retries.\\\";\\n163: \\n164:     /** <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> */\\n165:     public static final String DELIVERY_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG = \\\"delivery.timeout.ms\\\";\\n166:     private static final String DELIVERY_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC = \\\"An upper bound on the time to report success or failure \\\"\\n167:             + \\\"after a call to <code>send()</code> returns. a value greater than zero will cause the client to resend any record whose send fails with a potentially transient error. \\\"\\n279:             + \\\" Requests will be retried this many times until }\\n554: \\n555:     @Override\\n556:     protected Map<String, Object> postProcessParsedConfig(final Map<String, Object> parsedValues) {\\n557:         CommonClientConfigs.postValidateSaslMechanismConfig(this);\\n558:         CommonClientConfigs.warnDisablingExponentialBackoff(this);\\n559:         Map<String, Object> refinedConfigs = CommonClientConfigs.postProcessReconnectBackoffConfigs(this, parsedValues);\\n560:         postProcessAndValidateIdempotenceConfigs(refinedConfigs);\\n561:         maybeOverrideClientId(refinedConfigs);\\n562:         return refinedConfigs;\\n563:     }\\n564: \\n565:     private void maybeOverrideClientId(final Map<String, Object> configs) {\\n566:         String refinedClientId;\\n567:         boolean userConfiguredClientId = this.originals().containsKey(CLIENT_ID_CONFIG);\\n568:         if (userConfiguredClientId) {\\n569:             refinedClientId = this.getString(CLIENT_ID_CONFIG);\\n570:         } else {\\n571:             String transactionalId = this.getString(TRANSACTIONAL_ID_CONFIG);\\n572:             refinedClientId = \\\"producer-\\\" + (transactionalId != null ? transactionalId : PRODUCER_CLIENT_ID_SEQUENCE.getAndIncrement());\\n573:         }\\n574:         configs.put(CLIENT_ID_CONFIG, refinedClientId);\\n575:     }\\n576: \\n577:     private void postProcessAndValidateIdempotenceConfigs(final Map<String, Object> configs) {\\n578:         final Map<String, Object> originalConfigs = this.originals();\\n579:         final String acksStr = parseAcks(this.getString(ACKS_CONFIG));\\n580:         configs.put(ACKS_CONFIG, acksStr);\\n581:         final boolean userConfiguredIdempotence = this.originals().containsKey(ENABLE_IDEMPOTENCE_CONFIG);\\n582:         boolean idempotenceEnabled = this.getBoolean(ENABLE_IDEMPOTENCE_CONFIG);\\n583:         boolean shouldDisableIdempotence = false;\\n584: \\n585:         // For idempotence producers, values for `retries` and `acks` and `max.in.flight.requests.per.connection` need validation\\n586:         if (idempotenceEnabled) {\\n587:             final int retries = this.getInt(RETRIES_CONFIG);\\n588:             if (retries == 0) {\\n589:                 if (userConfiguredIdempotence) {\\n590:                     throw new ConfigException(\\\"Must set \\\" + RETRIES_CONFIG + \\\" to non-zero when using the idempotent producer.\\\");\\n591:                 }\\n592:                 log.info(\\\"Idempotence will be disabled because {} is set to 0.\\\", RETRIES_CONFIG);\\n593:                 shouldDisableIdempotence = true;\\n594:             }\\n595: \\n596:             final short acks = Short.parseShort(acksStr);\\n597:             if (acks != (short) -1) {\\n598:                 if (userConfiguredIdempotence) {\\n599:                     throw new ConfigException(\\\"Must set \\\" + ACKS_CONFIG + \\\" to all in order to use the idempotent \\\" +\\n600:                         \\\"producer. Otherwise we cannot guarantee idempotence.\\\");\\n601:                 }\\n602:                 log.info(\\\"Idempotence will be disabled because {} is set to {}, not set to 'all'.\\\", ACKS_CONFIG, acks);\\n603:                 shouldDisableIdempotence = true;\\n604:             }\\n605: \\n606:             final int inFlightConnection = this.getInt(MAX_IN_FLIGHT_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION);\\n607:             if (MAX_IN_FLIGHT_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION_FOR_IDEMPOTENCE < inFlightConnection) {\\n608:                 throw new ConfigException(\\\"To use the idempotent producer, \\\" + MAX_IN_FLIGHT_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION +\\n609:                                           \\\" must be set to at most 5. Current value is \\\" + inFlightConnection + \\\".\\\");\\n610:             }\\n611:         }\\n612: \\n613:         if (shouldDisableIdempotence) {\\n614:             configs.put(ENABLE_IDEMPOTENCE_CONFIG, false);\\n615:             idempotenceEnabled = false;\\n616:         }\\n617: \\n618:         // validate `transaction.id` after validating idempotence dependant configs because `enable.idempotence` config might be overridden\\n619:         boolean userConfiguredTransactions = originalConfigs.containsKey(TRANSACTIONAL_ID_CONFIG);\\n620:         if (!idempotenceEnabled && userConfiguredTransactions) {\\n621:             throw new ConfigException(\\\"Cannot set a \\\" + ProducerConfig.TRANSACTIONAL_ID_CONFIG + \\\" without also enabling idempotence.\\\");\\n622:         }\\n623: \\n624:         // Validate that transaction.timeout.ms is not set when transaction.two.phase.commit.enable is true\\n625:         // In standard Kafka transactions, the broker enforces transaction.timeout.ms and aborts any\\n626:         // transaction that isn't completed in time. With two-phase commit (2PC), an external coordinator\\n627:         // decides when to finalize, so broker-side timeouts don't apply. Disallow using both.\\n628:         boolean enable2PC = this.getBoolean(TRANSACTION_TWO_PHASE_COMMIT_ENABLE_CONFIG);\\n629:         boolean userConfiguredTransactionTimeout = originalConfigs.containsKey(TRANSACTION_TIMEOUT_CONFIG);\\n630:         if (enable2PC && userConfiguredTransactionTimeout) {\\n631:             throw new ConfigException(\\n632:                 \\\"Cannot set \\\" + ProducerConfig.TRANSACTION_TIMEOUT_CONFIG +\\n633:                 \\\" when \\\" + ProducerConfig.TRANSACTION_TWO_PHASE_COMMIT_ENABLE_CONFIG +\\n634:                 \\\" is set to true. Transactions will not expire with two-phase commit enabled.\\\"\\n635:             );\\n636:         }\\n637:     }\\n638: \\n639:     private static String parseAcks(String acksString) {\\n640:         try {\\n641:             return acksString.trim().equalsIgnoreCase(\\\"all\\\") ? \\\"-1\\\" : Short.parseShort(acksString.trim()) + \\\"\\\";\\n642:         } catch (NumberFormatException e) {\\n643:             throw new ConfigException(\\\"Invalid configuration value for 'acks': \\\" + acksString);\\n644:         }\\n645:     }\\n646: \\n647:     static Map<String, Object> appendSerializerToConfig(Map<String, Object> configs,\\n648:             Serializer<?> keySerializer,\\n649:             Serializer<?> valueSerializer) {\\n650:         // validate serializer configuration, if the passed serializer instance is null, the user must explicitly set a valid serializer configuration value\\n651:         Map<String, Object> newConfigs = new HashMap<>(configs);\\n652:         if (keySerializer != null)\\n653:             newConfigs.put(KEY_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG, keySerializer.getClass());\\n654:         else if (newConfigs.get(KEY_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG) == null)\\n655:             throw new ConfigException(KEY_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG, null, \\\"must be non-null.\\\");\\n656:         if (valueSerializer != null)\\n657:             newConfigs.put(VALUE_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG, valueSerializer.getClass());\\n658:         else if (newConfigs.get(VALUE_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG) == null)\\n659:             throw new ConfigException(VALUE_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG, null, \\\"must be non-null.\\\");\\n660:         return newConfigs;\\n661:     }\\n662: \\n663:     public ProducerConfig(Properties props) {\\n664:         super(CONFIG, props);\\n665:     }\\n666: \\n667:     public ProducerConfig(Map<String, Object> props) {\\n668:         super(CONFIG, props);\\n669:     }\\n670: \\n671:     public }\\n678: \\n679:     public static void main(String[] args) {\\n680:         System.out.println(CONFIG.toHtml(4, config -> \\\"producerconfigs_\\\" + config));\\n681:     }\\n682: \\n683: }\\n684: If the elapsed \" +\n77:             \"time since a topic was last produced to exceeds the metadata idle duration, then the topic's \" +\n78:             \"metadata is forgotten and the next access to it will force a metadata fetch request.\";\n79: \n80:     /** <code>batch.size</code> */\n81:     public static final String BATCH_SIZE_CONFIG = \"batch.size\";\n82:     private static final String BATCH_SIZE_DOC = \"The producer will attempt to batch records together into fewer requests whenever multiple records are being sent\"\n83:                                                  + \" to the same partition. This helps performance on both the client and the server. This configuration controls the \"\n84:                                                  + \"default batch size in bytes. \"\n85:                                                  + \"<p>\"\n86:                                                  + \"No attempt will be made to batch records larger than this size. \"\n87:                                                  + \"<p>\"\n88:                                                  + \"Requests sent to brokers will contain multiple batches, one for each partition with data available to be sent. \"\n89:                                                  + \"<p>\"\n90:                                                  + \"A small batch size will make batching less common and may reduce throughput (a batch size of zero will disable \"\n91:                                                  + \"batching entirely). A very large batch size may use memory a bit more wastefully as we will always allocate a \"\n92:                                                  + \"buffer of the specified batch size in anticipation of additional records.\"\n93:                                                  + \"<p>\"\n94:                                                  + \"Note: This setting gives the upper bound of the batch size to be sent. If we have fewer than this many bytes accumulated \"\n95:                                                  + \"for this partition, we will 'linger' for the <code>linger.ms</code> time waiting for more records to show up. \"\n96:                                                  + \"This <code>linger.ms</code> setting defaults to 5, which means the producer will wait for 5ms or until the record batch is \"\n97:                                                  + \"of <code>batch.size</code> (whichever happens first) before sending the record batch. Note that broker backpressure can \"\n98:                                                  + \" result in a higher effective linger time than this setting. \"\n99:                                                  + \"The default changed from 0 to 5 in Apache Kafka 4.0 as the efficiency gains from larger batches typically result in \"\n100:                                                  + \"similar or lower producer latency despite the increased linger.\";\n101: \n102:     /** <code>partitioner.adaptive.partitioning.enable</code> */\n103:     public static final String PARTITIONER_ADPATIVE_PARTITIONING_ENABLE_CONFIG = \"partitioner.adaptive.partitioning.enable\";\n104:     private static final String PARTITIONER_ADPATIVE_PARTITIONING_ENABLE_DOC =\n105:             \"When set to 'true', the producer will try to adapt to broker performance and produce more messages to partitions hosted on faster brokers. \"\n106:             + \"If 'false', the producer will try to distribute messages uniformly. Note: this setting has no effect if a custom partitioner is used.\";\n107: \n108:     /** <code>partitioner.availability.timeout.ms</code> */\n109:     public static final String PARTITIONER_AVAILABILITY_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG = \"partitioner.availability.timeout.ms\";\n110:     private static final String PARTITIONER_AVAILABILITY_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC =\n111:             \"If a broker cannot process produce requests from a partition for <code>\" + PARTITIONER_AVAILABILITY_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG + \"</code> time, \"\n112:             + \"the partitioner treats that partition as not available. This controls the \"\n125:                                            + \" durability of records that are sent. The following settings are allowed: \"\n126:                                            + \" <ul>\"\n127:                                            + \" <li><code>acks=0</code> If set to zero then the producer will not wait for any acknowledgment from the\"\n128:                                            + \" server at all. The record will be immediately added to the socket buffer and considered sent. No guarantee can be\"\n129:                                            + \" made that the server has received the record in this case, and the <code>retries</code> configuration will not\"\n130:                                            + \" take effect (as the client won't generally know of any failures). The offset given back for each record will\"\n131:                                            + \" always be set to <code>-1</code>.\"\n132:                                            + \" <li><code>acks=1</code> This will mean the leader will write the record to its local log but will respond\"\n133:                                            + \" without awaiting full acknowledgement from all followers. In this case should the leader fail immediately after\"\n134:                                            + \" acknowledging the record but before the followers have replicated it then the record will be lost.\"\n135:                                            + \" <li><code>acks=all</code> This means the leader will wait for the full set of in-sync replicas to\"\n136:                                            + \" acknowledge the record. This guarantees that the record will not be lost as long as at least one in-sync replica\"\n137:                                            + \" remains alive. This is the strongest available guarantee. This is equivalent to the acks=-1 setting.\"\n138:                                            + \"</ul>\"\n139:                                            + \"<p>\"\n140:                                            + \"Note that enabling idempotence requires this config value to be 'all'.\"\n141:                                            + \" If conflicting configurations are set and idempotence is not explicitly enabled, idempotence is disabled.\";\n142: \n143:     /** <code>linger.ms</code> */\n144:     public static final String LINGER_MS_CONFIG = \"linger.ms\";\n145:     private static final String LINGER_MS_DOC = \"The producer groups together any records that arrive in between request transmissions into a single batched request. \"\n146:                                                 + \"Normally this occurs only under load when records arrive faster than they can be sent out. However in some circumstances the client may want to \"\n147:                                                 + \"reduce the number of requests even under moderate load. This setting accomplishes this by adding a small amount \"\n148:                                                 + \"of artificial delay&mdash;that is, rather than immediately sending out a record, the producer will wait for up to \"\n149:                                                 + \"the given delay to allow other records to be sent so that the sends can be batched together. This can be thought \"\n150:                                                 + \"of as analogous to Nagle's algorithm in TCP. This setting gives the upper bound on the delay for batching: once \"\n151:                                                 + \"we get <code>\" + BATCH_SIZE_CONFIG + \"</code> worth of records for a partition it will be sent immediately regardless of this \"\n152:                                                 + \"setting, however if we have fewer than this many bytes accumulated for this partition we will 'linger' for the \"\n153:                                                 + \"specified time waiting for more records to show up. This setting defaults to 5 (i.e. 5ms delay). Increasing <code>\" + LINGER_MS_CONFIG + \"=50</code>, \"\n154:                                                 + \"for example, would have the effect of reducing the number of requests sent but would add up to 50ms of latency to records sent in the absence of load.\"\n155:                                                 + \"The default changed from 0 to 5 in Apache Kafka 4.0 as the efficiency gains from larger batches typically result in \"\n156:                                                 + \"similar or lower producer latency despite the increased linger.\";\n157: \n158:     /** <code>request.timeout.ms</code> */\n159:     public static final String REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG = CommonClientConfigs.REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG;\n160:     private static final String REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC = CommonClientConfigs.REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC\n161:         + \" This should be larger than <code>replica.lag.time.max.ms</code> (a broker configuration)\"\n162:         + \" to reduce the possibility of message duplication due to unnecessary producer retries.\";\n163: \n164:     /** <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> */\n165:     public static final String DELIVERY_TIMEOUT_MS_CONFIG = \"delivery.timeout.ms\";\n166:     private static final String DELIVERY_TIMEOUT_MS_DOC = \"An upper bound on the time to report success or failure \"\n167:             + \"after a call to <code>send()</code> returns. This limits the total time that a record will be delayed \"\n168:             + \"prior to sending, the time to await acknowledgement from the broker (if expected), and the time allowed \"\n169:             + \"for retriable send failures. produced. Available options are:\" +\n312:             \"<ul>\" +\n313:             \"<li>If not set, the default partitioning logic is used. \" +\n314:             \"This strategy send records to a partition until at least \" + BATCH_SIZE_CONFIG + \" bytes is produced to the partition. It works with the strategy:\" +\n315:             \"<ol>\" +\n316:             \"<li>If no partition is specified but a key is present, choose a partition based on a hash of the key.</li>\" +\n317:             \"<li>If no partition or key is present, choose the sticky partition that changes when at least \" + BATCH_SIZE_CONFIG + \" bytes are produced to the partition.</li>\" +\n318:             \"</ol>\" +\n319:             \"</li>\" +\n320:             \"<li><code>org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.RoundRobinPartitioner</code>: A partitioning strategy where \" +\n321:             \"each record in a series of consecutive records is sent to a different partition, regardless of whether the 'key' is provided or not, \" +\n322:             \"until partitions run out and the process starts over again. Note: There's a known issue that will cause uneven distribution when a new batch is created. \" +\n323:             \"See KAFKA-9965 for more detail.\" +\n324:             \"</li>\" +\n325:             \"</ul>\" +\n326:             \"<p>Implementing the <code>org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.Partitioner</code> interface allows you to plug in a custom partitioner.\";\n327: \n328:     /** <code>interceptor.classes</code> */\n329:     public static final String INTERCEPTOR_CLASSES_CONFIG = \"interceptor.classes\";\n330:     public static final String INTERCEPTOR_CLASSES_DOC = \"A list of classes to use as interceptors. \"\n331:                                                         + \"Implementing the <code>org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.ProducerInterceptor</code> interface allows you to intercept (and possibly mutate) the records \"\n332:                                                         + \"received by the producer before they are published to the Kafka cluster. private void postProcessAndValidateIdempotenceConfigs(final Map<String, Object> configs) {\n578:         final Map<String, Object> originalConfigs = this.originals();\n579:         final String acksStr = parseAcks(this.getString(ACKS_CONFIG));\n580:         configs.put(ACKS_CONFIG, acksStr);\n581:         final boolean userConfiguredIdempotence = this.originals().containsKey(ENABLE_IDEMPOTENCE_CONFIG);\n582:         boolean idempotenceEnabled = this.getBoolean(ENABLE_IDEMPOTENCE_CONFIG);\n583:         boolean shouldDisableIdempotence = false;\n584: \n585:         // For idempotence producers, values for `retries` and `acks` and `max.in.flight.requests.per.connection` need validation\n586:         if (idempotenceEnabled) {\n587:             final int retries = this.getInt(RETRIES_CONFIG);\n588:             if (retries == 0) {\n589:                 if (userConfiguredIdempotence) {\n590:                     throw new ConfigException(\"Must set \" + RETRIES_CONFIG + \" to non-zero when using the idempotent producer.\");\n591:                 }\n592:                 log.info(\"Idempotence will be disabled because {} is set to 0.\", RETRIES_CONFIG);\n593:                 shouldDisableIdempotence = true;\n594:             }\n595: \n596:             final short acks = Short.parseShort(acksStr);\n597:             if \" +\n600:                         \"producer. Otherwise we cannot guarantee idempotence.\");\n601:                 }\n602:                 log.info(\"Idempotence will be disabled because {} is set to {}, not set to 'all'.\", ACKS_CONFIG, acks);\n603:                 shouldDisableIdempotence = true;\n604:             }\n605: \n606:             final int inFlightConnection = this.getInt(MAX_IN_FLIGHT_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION);\n607:             if (MAX_IN_FLIGHT_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION_FOR_IDEMPOTENCE < inFlightConnection) {\n608:                 throw new ConfigException(\"To use the idempotent producer, \" + MAX_IN_FLIGHT_REQUESTS_PER_CONNECTION +\n609:                                           \" must be set to at most 5. Current value is \" + inFlightConnection + \".\");\n610:             }\n611:         }\n612: \n613:         if (shouldDisableIdempotence) {\n614:             configs.put(ENABLE_IDEMPOTENCE_CONFIG, false);\n615:             idempotenceEnabled = false;\n616:         }\n617: \n618:         // validate `transaction.id` after validating idempotence dependant configs because `enable.idempotence` config might be overridden\n619:         boolean userConfiguredTransactions = originalConfigs.containsKey(TRANSACTIONAL_ID_CONFIG);\n620:         if (!idempotenceEnabled && userConfiguredTransactions) {\n621:             throw new ConfigException(\"Cannot set a \" + ProducerConfig.TRANSACTIONAL_ID_CONFIG + \" without also enabling idempotence.\");\n622:         }\n623: \n624:         // Validate that transaction.timeout.ms is not set when transaction.two.phase.commit.enable is true\n625:         // In standard Kafka transactions, the broker enforces transaction.timeout.ms and aborts any\n626:         // transaction that isn't completed in time. With two-phase commit (2PC), an external coordinator\n627:         // decides when to finalize, so broker-side timeouts don't apply. Disallow using both.\n628:         boolean enable2PC = this.getBoolean(TRANSACTION_TWO_PHASE_COMMIT_ENABLE_CONFIG);\n629:         boolean userConfiguredTransactionTimeout = originalConfigs.containsKey(TRANSACTION_TIMEOUT_CONFIG);\n630:         if (enable2PC && userConfiguredTransactionTimeout) {\n631:             throw new ConfigException(\n632:                 \"Cannot set \" + ProducerConfig.TRANSACTION_TIMEOUT_CONFIG +\n633:                 \" when \" + ProducerConfig.TRANSACTION_TWO_PHASE_COMMIT_ENABLE_CONFIG +\n634:                 \" is set to true. Transactions will not expire with two-phase commit enabled.\"\n635:             );\n636:         }\n637:     }\n638: \n639:     private static String parseAcks(String acksString) {\n640:         try {\n641:             return acksString.trim().equalsIgnoreCase(\"all\") ? \"-1\" : Short.parseShort(acksString.trim()) + \"\";\n642:         } catch (NumberFormatException e) {\n643:             throw new ConfigException(\"Invalid configuration value for 'acks': \" + acksString);\n644:         }\n645:     }\n646: \n647:     static Map<String, Object> appendSerializerToConfig(Map<String, Object> This is required in order to be able to restore the\\n68:     producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, for example, then after a partition leader failure, the producer might see an OutOfSequence error. The base sequence number must\\n69:     be preserved for duplicate checking (the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer). As a result,\\n70:     it is possible to have empty batches in the log when all the records in the batch are cleaned but batch is still retained in order to preserve a producer's last sequence number.     <p>\\n159:     Batching is one of the big drivers of efficiency, and to enable batching the Kafka producer will attempt to accumulate data in memory and to send out larger batches in a single request. The batching can be configured\\n160:     to accumulate no more than a fixed number of messages and to wait no longer than some fixed latency bound (say 64k or 10 ms). This allows the accumulation of more bytes to send, and few larger I/O operations on the\\n161:     servers. This buffering is configurable and gives a mechanism to trade off a small amount of additional latency for better throughput.\\n162:     <p>\\n163:     Details on <a href=\\\"#producerconfigs\\\">configuration</a> and the <a href=\\\"#producerapi\\\">api</a> for the producer can be found\\n164:     elsewhere in the documentation.\\n165: \",\"endLine\":165,\"startLine\":158},{\"content\":\"329:     <p>\\n330:     The consumer configuration must include <code>isolation.level=read_committed</code> and <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>. The producer configuration must set <code>transactional.id</code>\\n331:     to the name of the transactional ID to be used, which configures the producer for transactional delivery and also makes sure that a restarted application causes any in-flight transaction from\\n332:     the previous instance to abort. Only the producer has the <code>transactional.id</code> configuration.\\n333:     <p>\\n334: \",\"endLine\":334,\"startLine\":329},{\"content\":\"347:         <li><strong>AbortableException</strong>: Exceptions that require transaction abort and reprocessing. These bubble up to the application, which must handle them by aborting the transaction and resetting the consumer position.</li>\\n348:         <li><strong>ApplicationRecoverableException</strong>: Exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The application must implement its own recovery strategy, which must include restarting the producer.</li>\\n349:         <li><strong>InvalidConfigurationException</strong>: Configuration-related exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The producer doesn't need to restart, but the application may choose to restart it.</li>\\n350:         <li><strong>KafkaException</strong>: General Kafka exceptions that don't fit into the above categories. This is required in order to be able to restore the\\n68:     producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, for example, then after a partition leader failure, the producer might see an OutOfSequence error. The base sequence number must\\n69:     be preserved for duplicate checking (the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer). As a result,\\n70:     it is possible to have empty batches in the log when all the records in the batch are cleaned but batch is still retained in order to preserve a producer's last sequence number.     <p>\\n159:     Batching is one of the big drivers of efficiency, and to enable batching the Kafka producer will attempt to accumulate data in memory and to send out larger batches in a single request. The batching can be configured\\n160:     to accumulate no more than a fixed number of messages and to wait no longer than some fixed latency bound (say 64k or 10 ms). This allows the accumulation of more bytes to send, and few larger I/O operations on the\\n161:     servers. This buffering is configurable and gives a mechanism to trade off a small amount of additional latency for better throughput.\\n162:     <p>\\n163:     Details on <a href=\\\"#producerconfigs\\\">configuration</a> and the <a href=\\\"#producerapi\\\">api</a> for the producer can be found\\n164:     elsewhere in the documentation.\\n165: \",\"endLine\":165,\"startLine\":158},{\"content\":\"329:     <p>\\n330:     The consumer configuration must include <code>isolation.level=read_committed</code> and <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>. The producer configuration must set <code>transactional.id</code>\\n331:     to the name of the transactional ID to be used, which configures the producer for transactional delivery and also makes sure that a restarted application causes any in-flight transaction from\\n332:     the previous instance to abort. Only the producer has the <code>transactional.id</code> configuration.\\n333:     <p>\\n334: \",\"endLine\":334,\"startLine\":329},{\"content\":\"347:         <li><strong>AbortableException</strong>: Exceptions that require transaction abort and reprocessing. These bubble up to the application, which must handle them by aborting the transaction and resetting the consumer position.</li>\\n348:         <li><strong>ApplicationRecoverableException</strong>: Exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The application must implement its own recovery strategy, which must include restarting the producer.</li>\\n349:         <li><strong>InvalidConfigurationException</strong>: Configuration-related exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The producer doesn't need to restart, but the application may choose to restart it.</li>\\n350:         <li><strong>KafkaException</strong>: General Kafka exceptions that don't fit into the above categories. These bubble up to the application for handling.</li>\\n351: \",\"endLine\":351,\"startLine\":347}],\"file\":\"docs/design.html\",\"repo\":\"github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489\",\"type\":\"file\",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489/-/blob/docs/design.html\"},{\"chunks\":[{\"content\":\"191:         </li>\\n192:         <li>A number of deprecated classes, methods, configurations and tools have been removed.\\n193:             <ul>\\n194: \",\"endLine\":194,\"startLine\":191},{\"content\":\"334:                         </li>\\n335:                         <li><code>kafka-configs.sh</code> now uses incrementalAlterConfigs API to alter broker configurations instead of the deprecated alterConfigs API,\\n336:                             and it will fall directly if the broker doesn't support incrementalAlterConfigs API, which means the broker version is prior to 2.3.x.\\n337: \",\"endLine\":337,\"startLine\":334},{\"content\":\"58:                     <li>\\n59:                         The configuration <code>log.cleaner.enable</code> is deprecated. [line truncated]\n126:     </p>\n127: ","endLine":127,"startLine":124},{"content":"67:     <p>On compaction, we preserve the first and last offset/sequence numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\n68:     producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, for example, then after a partition leader failure, the producer might see an OutOfSequence error. The base sequence number must\n69:     be preserved for duplicate checking (the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer). As a result,\n70:     it is possible to have empty batches in the log when all the records in the batch are cleaned but batch is still retained in order to preserve a producer's last sequence number. One oddity here is that the baseTimestamp\n71:     field is not preserved during compaction, so it will change if the first record in the batch is compacted away.</p>\n72: ","endLine":72,"startLine":67}],"file":"docs/implementation.html","repo":"github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489","type":"file","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489/-/blob/docs/implementation.html"},{"chunks":[{"content":"1099:         // 1. /**\n22:  * This exception is thrown if the producer cannot allocate memory for a record within max.block.ms due to the buffer\n23:  * being too full.\n24:  *\n25: ","endLine":25,"startLine":21},{"content":"16:  */\n17: package org.apache.kafka.clients.producer;\n18: \n19: ","endLine":19,"startLine":16},{"content":"32: \n33:     public ","endLine":35,"startLine":32}],"file":"clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/BufferExhaustedException.java","repo":"github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489","type":"file","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/BufferExhaustedException.java"},{"chunks":[{"content":"158:     <p>\n159:     Batching is one of the big drivers of efficiency, and to enable batching the Kafka producer will attempt to accumulate data in memory and to send out larger batches in a single request. The batching can be configured\n160:     to accumulate no more than a fixed number of messages and to wait no longer than some fixed latency bound (say 64k or 10 ms). This allows the accumulation of more bytes to send, and few larger I/O operations on the\n161:     servers. This buffering is configurable and gives a mechanism to trade off a small amount of additional latency for better throughput.\n162:     <p>\n163:     Details on <a href=\"#producerconfigs\">configuration</a> and the <a href=\"#producerapi\">api</a> for the producer can be found\n164:     elsewhere in the documentation.\n165: ","endLine":165,"startLine":158},{"content":"329:     <p>\n330:     The consumer configuration must include <code>isolation.level=read_committed</code> and <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>. The producer configuration must set <code>transactional.id</code>\n331:     to the name of the transactional ID to be used, which configures the producer for transactional delivery and also makes sure that a restarted application causes any in-flight transaction from\n332:     the previous instance to abort. Only the producer has the <code>transactional.id</code> configuration.\n333:     <p>\n334: ","endLine":334,"startLine":329},{"content":"347:         <li><strong>AbortableException</strong>: Exceptions that require transaction abort and reprocessing. These bubble up to the application, which must handle them by aborting the transaction and resetting the consumer position.</li>\n348:         <li><strong>ApplicationRecoverableException</strong>: Exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The application must implement its own recovery strategy, which must include restarting the producer.</li>\n349:         <li><strong>InvalidConfigurationException</strong>: Configuration-related exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The producer doesn't need to restart, but the application may choose to restart it.</li>\n350:         <li><strong>KafkaException</strong>: General Kafka exceptions that don't fit into the above categories. These bubble up to the application for handling.</li>\n351: ","endLine":351,"startLine":347}],"file":"docs/design.html","repo":"github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489","type":"file","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489/-/blob/docs/design.html"},{"chunks":[{"content":"191:         </li>\n192:         <li>A number of deprecated classes, methods, configurations and tools have been removed.\n193:             <ul>\n194: ","endLine":194,"startLine":191},{"content":"334:                         </li>\n335:                         <li><code>kafka-configs.sh</code> now uses incrementalAlterConfigs API to alter broker configurations instead of the deprecated alterConfigs API,\n336:                             and it will fall directly if the broker doesn't support incrementalAlterConfigs API, which means the broker version is prior to 2.3.x.\n337: ","endLine":337,"startLine":334},{"content":"58:                     <li>\n59:                         The configuration <code>log.cleaner.enable</code> is deprecated. The batching can be configured\\n160:     to accumulate no more than a fixed number of messages and to wait no longer than some fixed latency bound (say 64k or 10 ms). This allows the accumulation of more bytes to send, and few larger I/O operations on the\\n161:     servers. This buffering is configurable and gives a mechanism to trade off a small amount of additional latency for better throughput.\\n162:     <p>\\n163:     Details on <a href=\\\"#producerconfigs\\\">configuration</a> and the <a href=\\\"#producerapi\\\">api</a> for the producer can be found\\n164:     elsewhere in the documentation.\\n165: \",\"endLine\":165,\"startLine\":158},{\"content\":\"329:     <p>\\n330:     The consumer configuration must include <code>isolation.level=read_committed</code> and <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>. The batching can be configured\\n160:     to accumulate no more than a fixed number of messages and to wait no longer than some fixed latency bound (say 64k or 10 ms). This allows the accumulation of more bytes to send, and few larger I/O operations on the\\n161:     servers. This buffering is configurable and gives a mechanism to trade off a small amount of additional latency for better throughput.\\n162:     <p>\\n163:     Details on <a href=\\\"#producerconfigs\\\">configuration</a> and the <a href=\\\"#producerapi\\\">api</a> for the producer can be found\\n164:     elsewhere in the documentation.\\n165: \",\"endLine\":165,\"startLine\":158},{\"content\":\"329:     <p>\\n330:     The consumer configuration must include <code>isolation.level=read_committed</code> and <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>. The batching can be configured\n160:     to accumulate no more than a fixed number of messages and to wait no longer than some fixed latency bound (say 64k or 10 ms). This allows the accumulation of more bytes to send, and few larger I/O operations on the\n161:     servers. This buffering is configurable and gives a mechanism to trade off a small amount of additional latency for better throughput.\n162:     <p>\n163:     Details on <a href=\"#producerconfigs\">configuration</a> and the <a href=\"#producerapi\">api</a> for the producer can be found\n164:     elsewhere in the documentation.\n165: ","endLine":165,"startLine":158},{"content":"329:     <p>\n330:     The consumer configuration must include <code>isolation.level=read_committed</code> and <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>. These bubble up to the application, which must handle them by aborting the transaction and resetting the consumer position.</li>\n348:         <li><strong>ApplicationRecoverableException</strong>: Exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The application must implement its own recovery strategy, which must include restarting the producer.</li>\n349:         <li><strong>InvalidConfigurationException</strong>: Configuration-related exceptions that bubble up to the application and require application handling. The producer doesn't need to restart, but the application may choose to restart it.</li>\n350:         <li><strong>KafkaException</strong>: General Kafka exceptions that don't fit into the above categories.
